title: Node.js Streams & Backpressure
description: Readable, Writable, and Transform streams, `.pipe()` vs `stream.pipeline()`, what backpressure actually protects, and how object mode changes the rules. Every answer is verified by running the code in Node.

questions.title: A Transform stream is a specialized form of which base stream class?
questions.description: Name the stream class with both a readable and a writable side that Transform builds on.
questions.explanation:
A `Transform` extends `Duplex`. A `Duplex` has an *independent* readable side and writable side (think of a TCP socket: you read from it and write to it, and the two directions are unrelated). A `Transform` is the special case where the readable output is **computed from** the writable input — you write chunks in, a transformed version comes out.

You can confirm the hierarchy directly:

```js
const { Transform, Duplex } = require("node:stream");
console.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(Transform.prototype) === Duplex.prototype); // true
```

So the chain is `Transform → Duplex → (Readable + Writable)`. `zlib.createGzip()` and `crypto.createHash()` are everyday Transforms.

questions.title: What does `writable.write(chunk)` returning `false` tell you?
questions.description: A `write()` call returns `false`. Pick the correct interpretation.

options.text: The internal buffer has reached `highWaterMark`; you should stop writing and wait for the `'drain'` event.
The chunk was rejected and discarded — you must call `write()` again with the same chunk.
The stream has already been closed and will accept no more data.
The next `write()` will throw unless you pause first.

questions.explanation:
A `false` return is a **flow-control signal, not an error**. The chunk you just passed is still accepted and buffered — `false` only means the buffered amount has reached `highWaterMark`, so a well-behaved producer should pause and resume on `'drain'`.

Proof that the chunk is not dropped:

```js
const { Writable } = require("node:stream");
const w = new Writable({ highWaterMark: 4, write(c, e, cb) { setTimeout(cb, 1000); } });
console.log(w.write("zzzz")); // false  (buffered length 4 >= hwm 4)
console.log(w.writableLength);  // 4      (the chunk is buffered, not lost)
```

Ignoring `false` is legal — Node keeps buffering — but that is exactly how you defeat backpressure and grow memory without bound.

questions.title: What does backpressure in Node streams actually protect?
questions.description: Backpressure is the signal a slow consumer sends a fast producer. What resource is it guarding?

options.text: Memory — it stops a fast producer from growing the consumer's buffer without bound.
Ordering — it keeps chunks from arriving out of sequence.
CPU time — it caps how much work the event loop does per tick.
Bandwidth — it compresses data so fewer bytes travel over the network.

questions.explanation:
Backpressure is flow control whose job is to keep buffered (unconsumed) data from piling up in **process memory**. When a producer outruns a consumer, the unread chunks have to live *somewhere* — the stream's internal buffer, i.e. RAM. Backpressure is the feedback loop (`write()` returning `false`, then `'drain'`) that throttles the producer so the buffer hovers near `highWaterMark` instead of growing until the process runs out of heap.

It is *not* about ordering (streams already deliver chunks in order), nor CPU scheduling, nor bandwidth/compression. Picture a fast tap filling a slow-draining sink: backpressure turns the tap down so the sink never overflows — and the sink is your memory.

questions.title: Which statements about `.pipe()` and `stream.pipeline()` are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement. Both connect a readable to a writable, but they differ in lifecycle handling.

options.text: Both `.pipe()` and `pipeline()` apply backpressure automatically.
`pipeline()` destroys every stream in the chain and reports the failure to its callback or promise.
`.pipe()` returns the destination stream, so calls can chain: `a.pipe(b).pipe(c)`.
If the destination errors, `.pipe()` automatically destroys the source stream for you.

questions.title: When writing custom streams, which implementation rules are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement about the underscore-prefixed methods you implement.

options.text: A custom `Readable` implements `_read(size)` and emits data via `this.push(chunk)`.
A custom `Writable` implements `_write(chunk, encoding, callback)`.
A custom `Transform` implements `_transform(chunk, encoding, callback)` and may add `_flush(callback)`.
A custom `Transform` requires you to implement `_read` and `_write` yourself, separately.

questions.title: In object mode, what is a stream's default `highWaterMark`?
questions.description: Give the number of objects buffered by default when `objectMode: true` and no `highWaterMark` is set.
questions.explanation:
In object mode, `highWaterMark` is measured in **number of objects**, and the default is **16**. (In byte mode the default is a byte count — historically 16 KiB, raised to 64 KiB in Node 22+ — which is why memorizing the *byte* number is fragile, but the object-mode default of 16 has been stable.)

```js
const { Writable } = require("node:stream");
const w = new Writable({ objectMode: true, write(o, e, cb) { cb(); } });
console.log(w.writableHighWaterMark); // 16
```

So with the default, a producer can get ~16 objects ahead of a slow consumer before `write()` starts returning `false`.

questions.title: What does enabling `objectMode: true` change about a stream?
questions.description: Pick the accurate description of object-mode behavior.

options.text: `highWaterMark` counts objects instead of bytes, and each chunk passes through as a single value — never concatenated or split.
Every chunk is automatically serialized to JSON before it is written.
The stream delivers data synchronously instead of asynchronously.
Backpressure is turned off, because objects have no measurable byte length.

questions.explanation:
By default a stream moves `Buffer`/`string` data, `highWaterMark` is a byte count, and adjacent chunks may be merged or split. Set `objectMode: true` and the stream instead carries arbitrary JavaScript values (objects, arrays, numbers…), **one discrete value per chunk**, with `highWaterMark` becoming a **count of objects**. No JSON serialization happens — the actual reference flows through.

Backpressure still works; it just counts objects:

```js
const { Writable } = require("node:stream");
const w = new Writable({ objectMode: true, highWaterMark: 2, write(o, e, cb) { setImmediate(cb); } });
console.log(w.write({ n: 1 })); // true  (1 buffered, < 2)
console.log(w.write({ n: 2 })); // false (2 buffered, hwm reached)
console.log(w.write({ n: 3 })); // false
```

This is what lets object-mode streams power record-by-record pipelines (CSV rows, DB results) with the same flow control as byte streams.

questions.title: You write to a slow `Writable` in a tight loop and ignore every `false` return. What happens?
questions.description: No `'drain'` waiting, no pausing — just keep calling `write()`.

options.text: The internal buffer keeps growing past `highWaterMark` and memory climbs without bound — `highWaterMark` is advisory, not a hard cap.
`write()` throws a `RangeError` once the buffer exceeds `highWaterMark`.
Extra chunks are silently dropped to keep memory flat.
The event loop blocks until the consumer drains the buffer.

questions.explanation:
`highWaterMark` is a **threshold for the advisory signal, not an enforced ceiling**. Node will happily keep buffering everything you write; nothing throws, nothing is dropped, and the event loop never blocks (writes are non-blocking). The buffer just grows — straight toward an out-of-memory crash if the producer keeps winning.

```js
const { Writable } = require("node:stream");
const w = new Writable({ highWaterMark: 4, write(c, e, cb) { setTimeout(cb, 1000); } });
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) w.write("zzzz"); // all return false, all ignored
console.log(w.writableLength); // 20 — far past hwm of 4, no error
```

That is the whole point of honoring backpressure: respect `false`, wait for `'drain'`, and the buffer stays bounded. `.pipe()` and `pipeline()` do this for you.

questions.title: After `write()` returns `false`, which event signals it is safe to resume writing?
questions.description: You paused your producer on backpressure. What do you wait for?

options.text: `'drain'`
`'finish'`
`'ready'`
`'resume'`

questions.explanation:
`'drain'` fires once the buffered amount has fallen back below `highWaterMark`, meaning the writable can accept more data. The canonical backpressure-aware writer looks like:

```js
function writeOne(stream, data, done) {
  if (!stream.write(data)) stream.once("drain", done); // paused; resume on drain
  else process.nextTick(done);                          // still room; continue now
}
```

The distractors are real events that mean other things: `'finish'` fires after `end()` once all data is flushed (end of the *writable*), `'resume'` belongs to *readable* streams switching into flowing mode, and there is no generic `'ready'` event for resuming writes after backpressure.

questions.title: What is a Transform stream's `_flush(callback)` method for?
questions.description: When and why would you implement `_flush` on a custom Transform?

options.text: To emit any final buffered output after the last input chunk but before `'end'` — e.g. a compressor flushing its remaining bytes.
To discard the internal buffer immediately and free memory.
To force the operating system to flush the underlying file descriptor to disk.
To reset the stream so the same instance can be piped again.

questions.title: `fs.createReadStream(bigFile).pipe(res)` streams a huge file to a slow HTTP client. How is memory kept bounded?
questions.description: `res` is the writable HTTP response and the client reads slowly. What does `pipe()` do under the hood?

options.text: When `res.write()` returns `false`, `pipe()` pauses the readable and resumes it on `res`'s `'drain'`, so reads track the client's speed.
It buffers the entire file in memory first, then sends it all at once.
It drops the bytes the client cannot keep up with and continues.
It blocks the event loop until the client has consumed each chunk.

questions.explanation:
`pipe()` wires the two streams' flow control together. It watches the destination's `write()` return value: on `false` it calls `source.pause()` (stopping the file reads), and it listens for the destination's `'drain'` to call `source.resume()`. The result is that the file is read only as fast as the socket can send, so at most about one `highWaterMark` of data sits in memory regardless of file size — a 10 GB file can stream in roughly constant memory.

The distractors describe the failure modes backpressure prevents: buffering everything (memory blow-up), dropping data (corruption), or blocking the loop (Node never blocks on I/O). `pipeline(fs.createReadStream(bigFile), res, cb)` behaves the same way but also cleans up and reports errors.
